target.name: Stellaria
target.founder: Nicolas Breton (CEO); backed by CEA + Schneider Electric

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy

data.narrative:
Stellaria is a seed-stage nuclear energy company that has raised €23M and is backed by the CEA and Schneider Electric under CEO Nicolas Breton. The valuation analysis draws on a universe of 11 nuclear energy comparable companies: 3 public (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and 8 private peers including Oklo, TerraPower, X-Energy, Kairos Power, and Nano Nuclear Energy.

A peer-median approach was not applicable due to insufficient same-stage and adjacent-stage comparables within the nuclear energy sector. While 8 private peers operate in the same sector, the filtered set yielded fewer than three datapoints when screened for stage alignment and valuation tier similarity. A broader sector median would introduce material noise given the heterogeneity of nuclear business models and development timelines across the comparable set.

The analysis is constrained by data limitations at the seed stage for frontier nuclear technology. Neither traditional revenue multiples nor discounted cash flow methods are viable given the pre-revenue or early-revenue profile typical of this cohort. Triangulation across the public comp set (OKLO, NNE, SMR) offers directional reference points for the sector's risk-adjusted growth multiples, but direct calibration to Stellaria's stage and capital efficiency remains uncertain. The €23M raised to date provides a hard floor for enterprise value assessment, though the absence of market-derived valuation benchmarks for same-stage nuclear seed companies limits precision. Resolution of technical and regulatory risk remains the primary driver of upside.
